welcome.title: Understanding Health Anxiety
welcome.description: Ever wondered why health worries never go away — no matter how much you check? Here's exactly why.

cycle.title: This is What's Happening in Your Brain
cycle.description_1: Health anxiety doesn't just appear and leave. It runs in a loop — and every time you check, you make that loop stronger.
cycle.description_2: Each loop teaches your brain that the worry was worth checking. So it keeps coming back — each time a little louder.
cycle.highlight: 💡 Checking gives relief — but never freedom.

trigger.label: Trigger
trigger.desc: A headache. An article. A thought.

anxiety.label: Anxiety Spikes
anxiety.desc: What if something is wrong with me?

break_cycle.title: Here's How to Fight Back
break_cycle.description: Every stage of the cycle has a way to interrupt it. Tap each one to see what you can do:
tips: Notice it without reacting. Say to yourself — 'That's a trigger, not a fact.' Then take one slow breath before doing anything.
Name the feeling out loud — 'I'm feeling anxious right now.' Naming it reduces its power. You don't need to fix it — just acknowledge it.
This is the most important stage to interrupt. Delay the check by just 5 minutes. Then 10. Each delay weakens the habit.
Remind yourself — this relief won't last. Real relief comes from tolerating uncertainty, not escaping it.
Don't engage with the doubt. Say — 'There's that doubt again.' Then let it pass like a cloud without following it.
break_cycle.all_tapped: You now know exactly where the cycle lives — and how to interrupt it. That knowledge is powerful. 💙

brave_steps.pick_something_that_feels_like: Pick something that feels like a stretch — not impossible.
brave_steps.touch_a_surface_that_feels_unc:
Touch a surface that feels uncomfortable — and resist the urge to wash.
        Your brain learns the discomfort passes on its own.
brave_steps.touch_hold_anxiety_peaks_then_:
Touch → Hold → Anxiety peaks → Then drops. Each session trains your
          brain to feel safer.
brave_steps.pick_how_long_you_ll_hold_on: Pick how long you'll hold on.
brave_steps.discomfort_is_not_danger_the_u: Discomfort is not danger. The urge will peak — then pass.

guided_imagery.you_just_saw_even_for_a_moment:
You just saw — even for a moment — what a calmer space could feel like.
          That's not a fantasy. That's a possibility. You don't have to get there
          all at once. But now you know what you're moving towards.
guided_imagery.come_back_to_this_whenever_the: Come back to this whenever the work feels hard. It's waiting for you. ✨
guided_imagery.how_does_that_space_make_you_f: How does that space make you feel?
guided_imagery.whatever_you_feel_is_valid_the: Whatever you feel is valid. There is no wrong answer here. 🌿
guided_imagery.which_space_feels_most_overwhe: Which space feels most overwhelming right now?
guided_imagery.pick_the_one_that_comes_to_min: Pick the one that comes to mind first.
guided_imagery.drag_to_reveal_your_calm_space: Drag to reveal your calm space
guided_imagery.what_if_your_space_could_feel_: What if your space could feel different?
guided_imagery.this_exercise_shows_you_what_a:
This exercise shows you what a calm, clear space could look like — and
          helps you connect to how that actually feels. No imagination needed.
          Just slide and see.

Sometimes objects hold more than things. They hold feelings we haven't

Naming it doesn't mean you have to act on it. Awareness itself is the

welcome.title: Clutter & Emotion Journal
welcome.subtitle: "Every object you keep is keeping something for you."

welcome.placeholder: e.g. my mother's old scarf, a broken watch

exploration.title: Let's explore this together. 🔍
exploration.subtitle: Answer whichever feels right. You can skip any.
exploration.prompt1: When did this object come into your life? What was happening then?
exploration.prompt2: What do you imagine would happen if this object was gone tomorrow?
exploration.prompt3: Does this object remind you of a person, a time, or a version of yourself?
exploration.prompt4: What does keeping this object protect you from?
exploration.placeholder: Write freely — this is just for you. 💭

insight.title: What did you notice? 💡
insight.description1: Sometimes objects hold more than things. They hold feelings we haven't found another place for yet.
insight.description2: What is this object really holding for you?
insight.option2: A fear of not having enough
insight.option3: A memory I'm not ready to lose
insight.footer: Naming it doesn't mean you have to act on it. Awareness itself is the work. 🌸

closing.title: You just did something brave.
closing.subtitle: Most people never ask why they hold on. You did. That kind of honesty is where real change begins.
closing.footer: Come back whenever you're ready to explore another. 🌸

contamination_ocd.everyone_cares_about_cleanline: Everyone cares about cleanliness sometimes — but for some people, it becomes overwhelming.
contamination_ocd.contamination_ocd_is_a_form_of: Contamination OCD is a form of OCD where your mind gets stuck on the fear of germs, dirt, or getting sick. It's not just about being clean — it's an exhausting cycle where small things like touching a surface, shaking hands, or even thinking about germs can trigger intense worry. You may feel the need to wash, clean, or avoid things to feel safe. But even after doing that, the relief doesn't last long.
contamination_ocd.what_makes_it_ocd_and_not_just: What makes it OCD and not just being clean?
contamination_ocd.your_brain_is_not_broken_it_s_: Your brain is not broken — it's stuck in a loop. Contamination OCD feeds on fear and doubt, and the more you try to feel 100% safe, the stronger it gets. The good news is that with the right tools, you can break this cycle and feel more at ease again. 💙

did_you_know.knowledge_doesn_t_cure_trich_b: Knowledge doesn't cure trich. But it's where everything else begins.
did_you_know.things_your_brain_wishes_you_k: Things your brain wishes you knew
did_you_know.5_quick_facts_about_trich_no_j: 5 quick facts about trich — no jargon, no judgement.

discard_it.start_small_build_courage_each: Start small. Build courage. Each discard teaches your brain that letting go is safe.
discard_it.you_go_at_your_own_pace_nobody: 💙 "You go at your own pace. Nobody rushes you here."
discard_it.pick_5_items_we_ll_sort_them_b: Pick 5 items. We'll sort them by difficulty.
discard_it.start_from_the_bottom_work_up_: Start from the bottom. Work up slowly. 🌱
discard_it.find_one_pick_it_up_hold_it_in: Find one. Pick it up. Hold it in your hands.
discard_it.notice_what_comes_up_you_don_t: Notice what comes up. You don't have to make it go away.
discard_it.before_you_let_it_go_take_a_br: Before you let it go, take a breath.
discard_it.anxiety_is_high_and_that_s_oka: Anxiety is high — and that's okay.
discard_it.i_m_ready_to_let_it_go: I'm Ready to Let It Go →
discard_it.whatever_you_feel_you_did_it_t: "Whatever you feel — you did it. That's what matters."
discard_it.your_anxiety_came_down_that_s_: "Your anxiety came down. That's your brain learning it's safe to let go."
discard_it.every_time_you_do_this_it_gets: Every time you do this, it gets easier. Not because the items matter less — but because you matter more.
discard_it.every_rung_you_climb_is_proof_: "Every rung you climb is proof you are stronger than your fear."

fear_ladder.you_will_start_small_and_gradu: You will start small and gradually move upward. The goal is not to eliminate fear — but to practice staying present with it.
fear_ladder.we_ve_organized_your_fears_fro: We've organized your fears from lowest to highest. This path is designed for gradual success.
fear_ladder.today_you_will_face_this_speci: Today you will face this specific step from your ladder.
fear_ladder.built_with_care_for_your_thera: Built with care for your therapeutic journey. 🌱
fear_ladder.you_showed_up_and_faced_your_f: You showed up and faced your fears for 10 straight steps. This is the foundation of courage.
fear_ladder.this_is_only_an_example_to_hel: This is only an example to help you understand how to structure your own ladder.
fear_ladder.your_ladder_is_personal_start_: Your ladder is personal — start with what feels approachable today.
fear_ladder.this_fear_ladder_helps_you_pra: This fear ladder helps you practice facing situations OCD usually pushes you to avoid or fix.
fear_ladder.you_ll_start_with_small_manage: You'll start with small, manageable steps and gradually work upward.
fear_ladder.feeling_uncomfortable_or_uncer: Feeling uncomfortable or uncertain is expected — and safe to experience.
fear_ladder.the_goal_is_not_to_feel_calm_b: The goal is not to feel calm, but to practice staying present without responding.
fear_ladder.arrange_situations_from_least_: Arrange situations from least uncomfortable to most uncomfortable.
fear_ladder.what_fear_would_you_like_to_pr: What fear would you like to practice approaching — step by step?
fear_ladder.focus_on_learning_to_tolerate_: (Focus on learning to tolerate discomfort, not eliminating fear.)
fear_ladder.notice_what_shows_up_you_don_t: Notice what shows up. You don’t need to fix it.
fear_ladder.fear_of_dogs_contamination_che: Fear of dogs, contamination, checking, intrusive thoughts…
fear_ladder.you_showed_up_for_yourself_tod: You showed up for yourself today. That's the most important part of the journey.
fear_ladder.we_recommend_waiting_until_tom:
We recommend waiting until tomorrow for the next step.
                Giving your brain time to process today's exposure is a key part of the therapeutic process.
                Consistency matters more than speed.
fear_ladder.how_did_it_go_what_did_you_not: How did it go? What did you notice?
fear_ladder.complete_your_fear_ladder_abov: Complete your fear ladder above to see your progress here.
fear_ladder.each_step_you_take_is_a_step_t: Each step you take is a step toward freedom. You're building courage, not comfort.
fear_ladder.after_practicing_how_will_you_: After practicing, how will you reward yourself for showing up — regardless of how it feels?
fear_ladder.rewards_are_for_effort_not_for: Rewards are for effort, not for feeling calm.
fear_ladder.what_thought_image_or_urge_do_: What thought, image, or urge do you expect might show up while working on this?
fear_ladder.just_name_it_you_don_t_need_to: (Just name it. You don't need to solve or change it.)
fear_ladder.having_a_thought_doesn_t_make_: Having a thought doesn't make it true or important. Naming it is an act of courage, not agreement.
fear_ladder.take_your_time_building_your_f: Take your time building your fear ladder above. There's no rush.
fear_ladder.your_exposure_practice_will_be: Your exposure practice will begin from
fear_ladder.where_you_ll_work_through_your:
, 
            where you'll work through your ladder one step at a time.
fear_ladder.setting_up_thoughtfully_is_its: Setting up thoughtfully is itself an act of courage.
fear_ladder.here_s_your_step_for_today_tak: Here's your step for today. Take it at your own pace — showing up is what matters.
fear_ladder.progress_happens_through_repet: Progress happens through repetition, not perfection.

feelings_fact.when_ocd_shows_up_feelings_can: When OCD shows up, feelings can feel like facts.
feelings_fact.a_feeling_is_a_body_reaction: A feeling is a body reaction.
feelings_fact.it_can_include_anxiety_guilt_f: It can include anxiety, guilt, fear, or urgency.
feelings_fact.something_that_exists_outside_: Something that exists outside of fear.
feelings_fact.a_fact_remains_true_even_when_: A fact remains true even when anxiety changes.
feelings_fact.it_feels_like_i_might_lose_con: "It feels like I might lose control."
feelings_fact.if_i_had_this_thought_it_must_: "If I had this thought, it must mean something about me."
feelings_fact.a_thought_is_not_an_action: A thought is not an action.
feelings_fact.there_is_no_actual_evidence_of: "There is no actual evidence of danger right now."
feelings_fact.ocd_blends_feeling_and_fact_to: OCD blends feeling and fact together.
feelings_fact.because_it_feels_scary_it_must: "Because it feels scary, it must be true."
feelings_fact.you_don_t_need_to_solve_a_feel: You don't need to solve a feeling.
feelings_fact.instead_of_proving_the_thought: Instead of proving the thought wrong,
feelings_fact.this_is_a_feeling_not_a_fact: "This is a feeling, not a fact."
feelings_fact.you_don_t_need_to_eliminate_fe: You don't need to eliminate fear.
feelings_fact.you_only_need_to_recognise_it: You only need to recognise it.

tips: Notice the thought and label it gently: "This is an OCD thought."
Allow uncertainty instead of trying to solve it immediately.
Let uncomfortable feelings exist without judging yourself for having them.
Remind yourself that thoughts are mental events, not facts or intentions.
Focus on what you are doing in the present moment, not on "what if" scenarios.
Practice slow breathing when anxiety rises — inhale slowly, exhale even slower.
Keep a consistent sleep routine to support emotional regulation.
Eat regular, balanced meals to avoid anxiety spikes from low energy.
Reduce caffeine if you notice it increases restlessness or intrusive thoughts.
Be kind to yourself — progress in OCD recovery happens step by step, not perfectly.

grounded_techniques.Grounding techniques help bring your att: Grounding techniques help bring your attention back to the present moment.
grounded_techniques.Choose one activity that feels supportiv: Choose one activity that feels supportive right now.
grounded_techniques.Pick up or touch items near you: Pick up or touch items near you
Run your hands under cool or warm water.
grounded_techniques.Pay attention to the sensation on your s: Pay attention to the sensation on your skin.
Observe how it changes moment by moment.
grounded_techniques.Notice its texture, weight, and temperat: Notice its texture, weight, and temperature.
grounded_techniques.Is it smooth or rough? Heavy or light?: Is it smooth or rough? Heavy or light?
grounded_techniques.Let your focus stay fully on this one ob: Let your focus stay fully on this one object.
Take a slow breath in through your nose.
grounded_techniques.Notice the flavor, texture, and temperat: Notice the flavor, texture, and temperature.
Stay fully present with the experience.
Feel the sensation as you swallow.
Let yourself stay with that moment.
grounded_techniques.Notice the feeling of your feet touching: Notice the feeling of your feet touching the ground.
Let your breath match your steps.
Hold the ice in your hand.
grounded_techniques.Allow the strong sensation to anchor you: Allow the strong sensation to anchor you to the present.
Notice how it feels in your body.
grounded_techniques.Let the scent bring you into this moment: Let the scent bring you into this moment.
Shift your weight from side to side.
Notice how your body feels as it moves.
Let your attention rest on each one.
Notice areas of tension or ease.

hoarding_ocd.everyone_keeps_things_sometime: Everyone keeps things sometimes — but for some people, letting go feels really hard.
hoarding_ocd.hoarding_ocd_is_a_form_of_ocd_: Hoarding OCD is a form of OCD where your mind gets stuck on the fear of throwing something away that might be important. It's not just being messy or sentimental — it's an exhausting cycle where everyday items feel too valuable to lose. A simple object can trigger thoughts like "what if I need this later?" or "what if this matters?" And no matter how much you keep or reconsider — the doubt doesn't fully go away.
hoarding_ocd.what_makes_it_ocd_and_not_just: What makes it OCD and not just clutter?
hoarding_ocd.your_brain_is_not_broken_it_s_: Your brain is not broken — it's stuck in a loop. Hoarding OCD feeds on doubt and "what ifs," making everything feel important. The good news is that with the right tools, you can learn to let go and feel more at ease in your space and your mind. 💙

letter_to_ocd.you_ve_been_living_in_my_head_: You've been living in my head for too long. Today I'm writing back.
letter_to_ocd.this_is_your_space_write_whate:
This is your space. Write whatever you need to say — no rules, no
      structure. Just you and the page.
letter_to_ocd.you_don_t_get_to_run_my_life: You don't get to run my life.
letter_to_ocd.writing_this_took_courage_ever:
Writing this took courage. Every word you wrote back to OCD is a step
        toward reclaiming your life. 🌿
letter_to_ocd.every_time_you_wrote_back_to_o: Every time you wrote back to OCD.
letter_to_ocd.no_letters_yet_write_your_firs: No letters yet. Write your first one!

metta_heart_guide.regular_practice_transforms_ho:
Regular practice transforms how you relate to yourself and others, 
            creating ripples of compassion in your daily life.
metta_heart_guide.a_3_minute_journey_of_sending_: A 3-minute journey of sending love to yourself and others
metta_heart_guide.find_a_comfortable_position_an: Find a comfortable position and take a deep breath
metta_heart_guide.you_ve_sent_kindness_to_yourse:
You've sent kindness to yourself and the world. 
            Carry this warmth with you.
metta_heart_guide.meditation_this_ancient_practi:
meditation, this ancient practice 
            cultivates unconditional kindness toward yourself and all beings. Through gentle 
            phrases and heartfelt intention, you'll nurture inner peace and emotional balance.
metta_heart_guide.these_gentle_phrases_are_the_h:
These gentle phrases are the heart of Metta practice. Repeat them silently, 
            letting each word resonate with intention and care.
metta_heart_guide.adapt_these_phrases_to_words_t: Adapt these phrases to words that feel natural and meaningful to you.
metta_heart_guide.move_through_each_stage_at_you: Move through each stage at your own pace. There's no rush—this is your time for healing and connection.
metta_heart_guide.take_a_moment_to_notice_how_yo:
Take a moment to notice how you feel. Perhaps there's a warmth in your chest, 
              a softness in your face, or simply a quiet stillness. Whatever you notice is perfect.
metta_heart_guide.loving_kindness_doesn_t_end_wh:
Loving-kindness doesn't end when the meditation does. As you move through your day, 
              look for small moments to offer this same warmth—a smile to a stranger, patience with 
              yourself when things go wrong, understanding toward someone who frustrates you.
metta_heart_guide.try_offering_loving_kindness_p:
Try offering loving-kindness phrases during everyday moments—while waiting 
                    in line, before sleep, or when you first wake up. Even a few minutes can 
                    shift your entire day.
metta_heart_guide.remember_you_are_worthy_of_the:
Remember: You are worthy of the same kindness you so freely give to others. 
              May you be well. May you be at peace.
metta_heart_guide.thank_you_for_practicing_with_: Thank you for practicing with us. 🙏

cognitive_distortions.noticing_these_patterns_is_the: Noticing these patterns is the first step toward breaking them.
cognitive_distortions.swipe_or_tap_next_to_continue: Swipe or tap Next to continue

welcome.title: Mirror Work
welcome.quote: "The way you speak to yourself matters more than you know. This is a moment to be kind to the person who needs it most — you."
welcome.subtitle: Find a quiet moment. This is just for you.

mirror.title: Read These to Yourself
mirror.description: These are things your kindest, healthiest self wants you to hear. Read each one slowly — out loud if you can. Take your time.
mirror.read_again: Read it again if you need to. There is no rush.
mirror.completion_msg:
You showed up for yourself today.
That is everything. 💙
affirmations:
I am not my anxious thoughts.
My thoughts are not facts.

I am doing the best I can
with what I have right now.

It is okay to not have all the answers.
Uncertainty is part of life.

My body is not my enemy.
It is working hard to take care of me.

I deserve the same kindness
I would give to someone I love.

This worry will pass. It always does.
I have survived every hard moment so far.

I am more than my health anxiety.
There is so much more to me than this.

mood_tracker.title: How are you feeling?
mood_tracker.subtitle: Take a moment to check in with yourself

1.heading: Be Extra Kind Today 🤍
1.body: Struggling on a Monday is tough. Please remember you don't have to carry it all. Be gentle with yourself and know that showing up today is enough.

2.heading: Gently Navigating Monday ☁️
2.body: Feeling a bit 'Low' on a Monday is completely understandable. The start of the week can be heavy. Take things one step at a time today.

3.heading: Finding Your Monday Flow 🌊
3.body: An 'Okay' Monday is a win. You're showing up and easing into the week. Give yourself grace as you find your rhythm today.

4.heading: Steady and Strong Monday! ☀️
4.body: You're starting the week on a 'Good' note. That's a solid foundation. Keep this positive momentum going as you navigate your Monday tasks.

5.heading: Unstoppable Monday Energy! 🚀
5.body: Starting the week with a 'Great' mood is a superpower. You've set an incredible tone that will ripple through your entire week. Use this momentum to tackle your biggest goals today!

1.heading: Sending You Strength 🫂
1.body: Struggling on a Tuesday can feel isolating, but you're not alone. Reach out if you need to, and prioritize your peace above all else today.

2.heading: Patience with Yourself today 🌿
2.body: If Tuesday feels a bit 'Low', remember that progress isn't always linear. Listen to what your body and mind need right now.

3.heading: Tuesday Tranquility 🍃
3.body: An 'Okay' Tuesday is perfectly fine. You're keeping a steady pace. Take a moment to appreciate the small wins today.

4.heading: Tuesday's Looking Bright 🌤️
4.body: A 'Good' mood today is a gift. You're past the Monday hump and maintaining a positive outlook. Enjoy this steady energy!

5.heading: Tuesday Triumph! ✨
5.body: You're absolutely thriving today! Carrying that 'Great' energy into Tuesday means you're building real momentum. Keep shining!

1.heading: You're Not Alone Today 💜
1.body: Struggling in the middle of the week is hard. Give yourself permission to slow down. The weekend will be here before you know it.

2.heading: Midweek Softness ☁️
2.body: If Wednesday feels 'Low', try to find one tiny thing to look forward to this evening. You've worked hard this week; it's okay to feel tired.

3.heading: Midweek Balance ⚖️
3.body: An 'Okay' Wednesday is a steady anchor. You're balanced and moving forward. Take a deep breath — you're halfway there.

4.heading: Hump Day Happiness 🐫
4.body: You've made it to Wednesday with a 'Good' vibe. The weekend is coming into view! Keep that positive spirit as you cross the midweek line.

5.heading: Midweek Masterclass! 🏅
5.body: Halfway through the week and feeling 'Great'! You're crushing it. Use this mid-week peak to do something that makes you truly happy.

1.heading: Gentle Thursday Hug 🧸
1.body: Struggling on a Thursday is a sign the week has been long. Be your own best friend today. You're almost at the weekend finish line.

2.heading: Thursday Reflection 🍂
2.body: If you're feeling 'Low' today, look back at how much you've already accomplished this week. You've come so far; it's okay to be weary.

3.heading: Steady into Thursday 🕰️
3.body: An 'Okay' Thursday is a job well done. You've put in the work all week. Just a little further to go — keep that steady pace.

4.heading: Almost There, Thursday! 🌅
4.body: A 'Good' Thursday sets you up for a fantastic weekend. You can feel the finish line! Keep that smile going through today's finish.

5.heading: Thursday Thrill! 🎡
5.body: Feeling 'Great' with the weekend so close? That's the best! Finish your week strong with this incredible burst of energy.

1.heading: Healing Friday Energy ✨
1.body: Struggling as the week ends is a lot to handle. Be proud of yourself for making it to Friday. This weekend is for your recovery.

2.heading: Friday Release 🌊
2.body: If Friday feels 'Low', let yourself sink into rest tonight. You've carried a lot this week. It's time to put the heavy things down.

3.heading: Friday Ease 😌
3.body: An 'Okay' Friday means you've made it through. The pressure is lifting. Take tonight to unwind and let go of the week's stress.

4.heading: Friday Feeling Good! 🎊
4.body: A 'Good' Friday is the perfect bridge to the weekend. You've navigated the week successfully. Enjoy the transition into your time.

5.heading: Fantastic Friday! 🥳
5.body: Closing the week in a 'Great' mood — what a victory! You've earned this joy. Let the weekend celebrations begin with this spark!

1.heading: Comforting Saturday 🕯️
1.body: Struggling on a weekend can feel extra hard. Please be gentle with your heart today. There's no right way to feel, even on a Saturday.

2.heading: Saturday Softness 💤
2.body: If Saturday feels 'Low', don't feel pressured to 'do the weekend'. If you need to stay in and rest, that is a perfectly valid choice.

3.heading: Simple Saturday 🍵
3.body: An 'Okay' Saturday is a peaceful plateau. No rush, no pressure. Just existing is enough today. Enjoy the quiet moments.

4.heading: Serene Saturday 🏖️
4.body: Feeling 'Good' on your day off is a blessing. Whether you're active or resting, enjoy every moment of this positive weekend vibe.

5.heading: Saturday Splendor! 🌈
5.body: A 'Great' Saturday is pure magic. This is your time to shine and enjoy the freedom of the weekend. Make some beautiful memories today!

1.heading: Sunday Self-Care 💖
1.body: Struggling on a Sunday is more common than you think. Treat yourself with extreme tenderness today. You are worth the extra care.

2.heading: Sunday Scaries, Begone 🌙
2.body: If Sunday feels 'Low', try to focus on the present moment rather than tomorrow's work. You are safe and supported right now.

3.heading: Sunday Settling 🍂
3.body: An 'Okay' Sunday is a perfect time for reflection and prep. You're steady and ready for whatever comes next. Stay grounded.

4.heading: Gentle Sunday Vibes 🕊️
4.body: Feeling 'Good' today sets a peaceful tone for the week ahead. Enjoy the last bit of the weekend with a grateful heart.

5.heading: Sunday Sunshine! 🌻
5.body: A 'Great' Sunday is the ultimate recharge. You're heading into next week with a full heart. Savor this beautiful energy today!

mood_tracker.please_access_this_journal_thr: Please access this journal through the official link provided to you.
mood_tracker.append_a_token_to_your_url: Append a token to your URL:

ocd-treatment-guide.title: OCD Treatment: What You Need to Know

hero.p1: Being diagnosed with Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder can feel overwhelming. But it's important to know that OCD is highly treatable.
hero.p2: With the right support and evidence-based care, many people are able to regain control and live fulfilling lives.

is_treatable.title: Is OCD Actually Treatable?
is_treatable.p1: Yes — and there's strong research to support that.
is_treatable.p2: Roughly half of individuals respond very well to standard first-line treatments. Even if the first attempt doesn't work perfectly, about 70% of people see meaningful improvement when they stay engaged with treatment and adjust strategies as needed.
is_treatable.quote: Recovery doesn't always happen overnight, but progress is absolutely possible.

specialized.title: Specialized Therapy
specialized.subtitle: ERP-Based CBT
specialized.compulsions: repetitive behaviors done to reduce anxiety.
specialized.p2: The most effective therapy for OCD is a specific form of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy called Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP).
specialized.p3: Instead of avoiding anxiety triggers, ERP helps individuals:

steps.2: Resist performing the usual compulsive response
steps.3: Learn that anxiety naturally decreases without rituals

specialized.quote: Over time, this retrains the brain's fear response.
specialized.p4: Therapy is typically conducted weekly over several months. During this period, clients also practice exercises between sessions. While anxiety may temporarily increase during exposures, this discomfort is part of the healing process.
specialized.p5: Studies suggest that a majority of people who fully engage in ERP experience significant symptom reduction.

get_started.title: How Do You Get Started?
get_started.p1: The first step is reaching out to a healthcare professional — this could be a primary care provider, psychiatrist, or an OCD-trained therapist.
care_items: Consideration of your preferences and goals
goal_items: Building coping tools for stressful situations

final_word.title: A Final Word
final_word.p1: OCD can be persistent, but it is manageable. Many people — including clinicians, researchers, and individuals with lived experience — have dedicated their work to improving outcomes for those affected.
final_word.quote: With the right combination of treatment, patience, and support, meaningful recovery is possible.

insights.title: Response Insights
insights.subtitle: Your Growth Analytics
insights.log_entries: Log entries during this period to see your progress patterns here.
insights.info: Insights require at least 1 entry to show summary, and 4 entries for pattern analysis.
insights.pattern_resisted: You noticed urges without acting more often than you acted on them. That is proof of strength in observation.
insights.pattern_acted: You acted on urges more often this period. This is simply a phase of the journey — your awareness is the first step to change.
insights.pattern_balanced: Your responses were evenly balanced this period. You are finding your middle ground.

one_thing_out.One item. One day. One small act of lett: One item. One day. One small act of letting go.
one_thing_out.Not about decluttering. About practicing: Not about decluttering. About practicing the feeling of release.
one_thing_out.What thought comes up when you think abo: What thought comes up when you think about letting it go?
one_thing_out.I See My Thought → Let It Go: I See My Thought → Let It Go
one_thing_out.That wasn't just an object. That was you: That wasn't just an object. That was you practicing trust — that you'll be okay without it.
one_thing_out.Something from a drawer you haven't open: Something from a drawer you haven't opened in months
one_thing_out.Something you've been keeping just in ca: Something you've been keeping just in case

one_thing_out.Something that belongs to a past version: Something that belongs to a past version of you

one_thing_out.Don't overthink it. The first thing that: Don't overthink it. The first thing that comes to mind is usually the right one.
one_thing_out.Noticing the thought is the practice. Yo: Noticing the thought is the practice. You don't have to fix it.

pure_ocd.the_thoughts_feel_unbearable_b: "The thoughts feel unbearable. But having them does not make you a bad person — not even close."
pure_ocd.pure_o_ocd_is_a_form_of_ocd_wh: Pure O OCD is a form of OCD where the obsessions happen entirely in the mind. Unlike other types of OCD, there are no visible rituals — no washing, no checking, no arranging. Instead the compulsions are mental — replaying thoughts, seeking reassurance, mentally reviewing past events, or trying to neutralise a thought by replacing it with a 'good' one. It is exhausting in a way that is very hard to explain to others.
pure_ocd.what_kind_of_thoughts_does_pur: What kind of thoughts does Pure O bring?
pure_ocd.pure_o_ocd_is_one_of_the_most_: Pure O OCD is one of the most misunderstood and underdiagnosed forms of OCD. Many people suffer in silence for years, convinced that their thoughts mean something terrible about them. They don't. What makes Pure O OCD so cruel is that it targets the things you care about most — your relationships, your values, your sense of self. That is the nature of OCD, not the nature of you.
pure_ocd.your_mind_is_not_your_enemy_it: "Your mind is not your enemy — it's just stuck in a loop. With the right support and tools, you can learn to let these thoughts pass without giving them power. You are so much more than the thoughts your brain throws at you. 💙"

intro.title: Let's look at the thought differently.
intro.p1: Our minds generate automatic thoughts — especially when anxiety is high.
intro.p2: Some feel urgent. Some feel convincing.
intro.p3: But thoughts are interpretations, not facts.
intro.p4: Today, we'll practice gently reshaping one.

identify.title: What was the thought?
identify.p1: Write the thought exactly as it appeared in your mind.
identify.placeholder: e.g., "If I make a mistake, everything will fall apart."
identify.hint: Notice the tone of the thought.

examine.title: Let's slow it down.
examine.question: What makes this thought feel true? Is it:
examine.hint: Anxiety often amplifies possibilities into certainties.

reframe.title: What is a more balanced version?
reframe.p1: This is not about pretending everything is fine.
reframe.list_title: Try writing a thought that is:
reframe.example_original: If I think it, it must be true.
reframe.example_balanced: Thoughts happen automatically. They don't define my actions.

integration.title: Notice the shift.
integration.p1: Compare the original thought and the reframed one.

distortions.feeling: A feeling disguised as a fact

response_guide.give_your_hands_something_else: Give your hands something else to do
response_guide.when_the_urge_to_pull_arrives_: When the urge to pull arrives, your hands want to move. This gives them a job — one that makes pulling physically impossible.
response_guide.pick_a_move_practice_it_use_it: Pick a move. Practice it. Use it every time the urge arrives.
response_guide.pick_the_one_that_feels_most_n: Pick the one that feels most natural. You can change it anytime.
response_guide.this_is_your_anchor_use_it_eve: This is your anchor. Use it every time the urge arrives.
response_guide.every_second_you_hold_is_a_sec: Every second you hold is a second the urge is passing.
response_guide.your_hands_know_what_to_do: Your hands know what to do
response_guide.you_just_gave_your_hands_somet: You just gave your hands something else to do — and held it. Next time the urge arrives, go straight to your move.
response_guide.the_faster_you_reach_for_it_th: The faster you reach for it, the easier it gets.

self_compassion.three_small_moments_a_breath_a: Three small moments. A breath, a name, a kind word. That's enough to interrupt the shame cycle.
self_compassion.and_you_chose_kindness_anyway_: And you chose kindness anyway. That's the practice.

thought_diffusion.no_sessions_yet_start_your_fir: No sessions yet. Start your first one!
thought_diffusion.your_mind_is_very_good_at_maki: Your mind is very good at making thoughts feel like facts. But a thought is just a thought — not a command, not a truth, not a reflection of who you are. In this exercise, you'll practice Defusion — stepping back from intrusive thoughts instead of fighting them.
thought_diffusion.when_your_mind_fuses_with_a_th: When your mind fuses with a thought, it feels real and urgent. Defusion creates distance — without pushing the thought away.
thought_diffusion.i_am_having_the_thought_that_i: ✅ "I am having the thought that I might hurt someone."
thought_diffusion.that_one_small_shift_changes_e: That one small shift changes everything.

thought_diffusion.this_reminds_your_brain_this_i: This reminds your brain: this is a thought, not a fact.

thought_diffusion.you_re_not_agreeing_with_the_t: 🕊️ You're not agreeing with the thought — just observing it.
thought_diffusion.looking_at_intrusive_thoughts_: Looking at intrusive thoughts — even from a small distance — takes real courage.
thought_diffusion.you_are_not_your_thoughts_you_: "You are not your thoughts. You are the one noticing them." 🌿
thought_diffusion.after_using_the_defusion_phras: After using the defusion phrase, the thought felt…

thought_surfing.exercise_you_will_visualize_yo:
exercise, you will visualize yourself
            resting near a stream. Whenever thoughts enter your mind, you will imagine placing
            them atop a leaf, and watching as they float away. Instead of seeing the world from
            "inside" your thoughts, you will take a step back and view them from afar. By doing
            so, you will gain perspective on your thoughts and feelings, reducing their power.
thought_surfing.when_you_re_ready_notice_a_tho:
When you're ready, notice a thought or feeling. Type it below, place it on a leaf,
            and watch it drift away.

welcome.title: Thought or Truth?
welcome.description: Swipe left for False / Swipe right for True!

before.title: Before we start
before.p1: Our brain creates all kinds of thoughts every day — even scary or weird ones. That doesn't mean anything is wrong with you.
before.p2: With Pure O OCD, the thought itself is not the problem. The problem is how much importance we give it.

results.remember: Remember: thoughts are just thoughts. They don't define who you are. 💜

questions.q: Having a violent thought means you're a dangerous person.
questions.correctExplain: Thoughts are not facts. Everyone has intrusive thoughts — they don't define you.
questions.wrongExplain: Actually, having a scary thought doesn't make you dangerous. Intrusive thoughts are normal.

questions.q: Intrusive thoughts are experienced by almost everyone at some point.
questions.correctExplain: Research shows 90%+ of people experience intrusive thoughts. You're not alone!
questions.wrongExplain: Intrusive thoughts are actually very common — studies show almost everyone has them.

questions.q: The more you try to suppress a thought, the more it comes back.
questions.correctExplain: This is called the 'white bear effect.' Suppression makes thoughts stickier.
questions.wrongExplain: It's actually true! Trying to push thoughts away makes them bounce back stronger.

questions.q: If a thought feels real, it must be true.
questions.correctExplain: Feelings aren't facts. OCD makes thoughts feel urgent and real — but they're just thoughts.
questions.wrongExplain: Just because a thought feels real doesn't make it true. OCD is tricky that way.

questions.q: Accepting a thought is the same as agreeing with it.
questions.correctExplain: Acceptance means letting a thought exist without fighting it — not endorsing it.
questions.wrongExplain: Acceptance ≠ agreement. You can notice a thought without believing or acting on it.

tricho_ocd.you_re_not_doing_it_on_purpose: You're not doing it on purpose. You're not 'just a habit.'
tricho_ocd.and_you_are_definitely_not_alo: And you are definitely not alone.
tricho_ocd.trichotillomania_is_a_conditio:
Trichotillomania is a condition where a person feels powerful, often
          irresistible urges to pull out their own hair — from the scalp,
          eyebrows, eyelashes, or anywhere else on the body. For some it happens
          consciously, for others it happens almost automatically without even
          realising it. Either way, it's not a choice and it's not a character
          flaw.
tricho_ocd.it_shows_up_differently_for_ev: It shows up differently for everyone:
tricho_ocd.trichotillomania_is_classified:
Trichotillomania is classified as a Body-Focused Repetitive Behaviour
          (BFRB). It often begins in childhood or adolescence and tends to get
          more intense during times of stress, boredom or emotional overwhelm.
          Many people spend years feeling ashamed without ever knowing there's a
          name for what they experience — or that help exists.
tricho_ocd.your_brain_has_learned_that_pu:
Your brain has learned that pulling brings relief. But that relief is
            temporary — and the cycle always comes back. The good news is that
            with the right tools and practice, you can gently interrupt this
            pattern and build a new relationship with these urges. 💙

uncertainity_acceptance.a_quiet_practice_of_sitting_wi: A quiet practice of sitting with a doubt — and choosing not to resolve it. Just for now.

screen1.label: URGE SURFING
screen1.title: You don't have to pull
screen1.body1: That familiar pulling feeling is here. You don't have to fight it, and you don't have to give in. There's a third way — ride it out.
screen1.body2: Every pulling urge builds, peaks, and fades on its own — just like a wave. It feels urgent right now, but it won't stay this intense. Let's watch it pass together.

screen2.title: Find the pull
screen2.body: Where do you feel it — scalp, brows, lashes? Is it tingly, itchy, tight? Just notice it. Don't feed it.

screen3.title: Watch it rise… and fall
screen3.body: The urge is peaking now. The tingle, the pull, the need to just do it — this is the loudest it gets. Stay here. Keep breathing. Your fingers might want to move — just notice that without following it.
screen3.footer: You are not the urge. You're the one watching it.

screen4.title: You rode the wave
screen4.body: The pull is easing now. You didn't fight it. You didn't give in. You just stayed — and that was enough.
screen4.reflection_label: Did the urge shift at all? What helped you stay?
screen4.footer: The more you practice, the smaller the waves get.

screen5.title: Your history
screen5.empty_message:
You haven't surfed any waves yet.
Your sessions will appear here.

what_is_health_ocd.we_all_worry_about_our_health_: We all worry about our health from time to time — but for some people, that worry just doesn't go away.
what_is_health_ocd.health_ocd_is_when_your_brain_: Health OCD is when your brain gets stuck on the idea that something is seriously wrong with your body. It's more than just being a little worried — it's an exhausting loop. A tiny feeling in your body, a random thought, or even a word you hear can send your mind racing with "what if I'm really sick?" You might check your body, search online, or ask someone for comfort — but the relief never sticks. The worry always comes back.
what_is_health_ocd.how_is_this_different_from_nor: How is this different from normal worry?
what_is_health_ocd.there_s_nothing_wrong_with_you: There's nothing wrong with your brain — it's just stuck in a loop. Health OCD feeds on uncertainty. The more you try to feel 100% sure you're okay, the stronger it gets. But here's the good news: with the right help, you can break free from this cycle and stop living in constant fear about your health. 💙
